plainMeaning: At that same mountain (Mount Ābu) a second grand Jina-temple, vast and dazzlingly lovely, was built by the minister Śrī Vastupāla.
recitation: aïramma-maï visālaṁ, maḍhicchiyaṁ sūrakhaṁ va paḍihāi | varajiṇa bhavaṇaṁ bīaṁ, tattha siri vatthupāla kayaṁ ||

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. from Skt. ati-ramya (ati 'very' + ramya 'delightful, charming')
wordByWord.notes: Joins with the following maï as the compound aïramma-maï 'made of utter loveliness'.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt./Apabhraṁśa suffix -maï from Skt. -maya 'made of, full of'
wordByWord.notes: Read with aïramma: 'consisting wholly of (supreme) beauty'. Could alternatively be read as a separate word, but the -maya suffix fits the syntax best.

wordByWord.etymology: Likely maḍha- (cf. Guj. માઢવું/મઢવું 'to overlay, encrust, inlay') + icchiya (Skt. īpsita/icchita 'desired'); i.e. 'fashioned and adorned to the wish'
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain; see verse-level notes. An alternative parse mano-icchiyaṁ ('desired in the mind') is possible but the orthography ઢ (ḍha) favours the maḍha- 'overlay/encrust' sense.

wordByWord.etymology: sūra (Skt. sūrya 'sun'; note long ū) + khaṁ (Skt. kha 'sky, space')
wordByWord.notes: The long ū points to sūrya 'sun' rather than sura 'god'; the white-marble shrine is likened to the sun blazing in the sky.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. va from Skt. iva, the particle of simile
Pkt. paḍihāi from Skt. pratibhāti (prati + √bhā 'to shine, appear'), 3rd-person singular present
vara 'best, excellent' (Skt. vara) + jiṇa (Skt. jina 'victor, Jina')
wordByWord.notes: Here the enshrined Jina is Neminātha, the 22nd Tīrthaṅkara, to whom the second Ābu temple is dedicated.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. bhavaṇa from Skt. bhavana 'dwelling, house, temple'

wordByWord.notes: Marks this as the second great Jina-temple at Ābu, after Vimala's shrine of the previous verse.
Refers back to Mount Ābu named in the preceding verse (v.92).

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. siri from Skt. śrī 'fortune, glory'; here an honorific prefix

wordByWord.notes: The famed 13th-century Prāgvāṭa (Porwad) minister of the Vāghela court; with his brother Tejapāla a great Jain temple-patron.

wordByWord.gloss: made, built, caused to be made
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. kaya from Skt. kṛta (√kṛ 'to do, make'), past passive participle

literalTranslation: Vast and made wholly of supreme loveliness, fashioned (and adorned) to the heart's wish, it shines like the sun in the sky — there a second temple of the excellent Jina was built by Śrī Vastupāla.
idiomaticTranslation: There too rose a second shrine to the noble Jina — immense, exquisite in every part, wrought to the heart's desire, gleaming like the very sun in the sky; this temple the illustrious Vastupāla caused to be built.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where v.92 honoured Vimala's first temple on Ābu, this verse turns to its companion — the 'second' (bīaṁ) great Jina-shrine, the marble Lūṇa-vasahī dedicated to Neminātha. The poet does not narrate; he paints. Four strokes build the image: visālaṁ (vast), aïramma-maï (made of nothing but loveliness), maḍhicchiyaṁ (wrought and inlaid exactly to one's heart's wish), and the climactic simile sūrakhaṁ va paḍihāi — 'it gleams like the sun in the open sky.' The comparison is precise: the Lūṇa-vasahī is carved from translucent white Makrana marble, so fine that ceilings and pendants seem lit from within; against the blue hill-sky it does blaze like a fixed sun. Only after the eye has been dazzled does the verse name the patron — siri vatthupāla kayaṁ — placing the human deed last, as the cause behind the splendour. The hymn credits Vastupāla; epigraphy at the site assigns the actual foundation (1230 CE) to his brother Tejapāla, who raised it in memory of their brother Lūṇiga (hence Lūṇa-vasahī). Tradition remembers the two ministers together, so the single name stands for the family's joint merit. The verse thus advances the Ābu sequence from the older Ādinātha shrine to the younger Neminātha shrine, and prepares vv.94–96, which dwell on this temple's banners, pinnacles and ornament — the very 'loveliness' here only announced.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse exemplifies tīrtha-bhakti expressed through dāna and temple-building (jina-bhavana-kāraṇa) — counted among the highest meritorious acts for a śrāvaka, since a shrine perpetuates darśana-pūjā for countless worshippers. By recording a layman-minister's gift, the hymn affirms that the householder's path bears fruit in lasting devotional works, not only in inward vows. The sun-simile carries quiet doctrine too: as the sun reveals all forms, the Jina-image and its shrine reveal the path — light here standing for jñāna (knowledge) dispelling darkness.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the worshipper performs bhāva-pūjā (mental worship) of a temple they may never physically visit — bowing to Ābu's Neminātha shrine from the prayer-hall. It also models the merit of supporting sacred works: the reciter is invited to honour patrons of dharma and to direct their own resources, however modest, toward preserving places of worship.

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā v.92 (Vimala-vasahī, the first Ābu temple) and vv.94–96 (this temple's ornament)
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Jaina inscriptions of the Neminātha (Lūṇa-vasahī) temple on Mount Ābu, dated VS 1287 / 1230 CE

sources.title: Jaina inscriptions of the temple of Neminatha on Mount Abu
sources.consultedFor: Foundation date (1230 CE), dedication to Neminātha, and the inscriptional attribution to Tejapāla (Lūṇa-vasahī named for brother Lūṇiga)

sources.title: Vastupala — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Vastupāla and Tejapāla as Vāghela-court Jain ministers and temple patrons

sources.title: Temples of Vastupala and Tejapala — Wisdom Library (The Structural Temples of Gujarat)
sources.consultedFor: Architectural description of the Lūṇa-vasahī and confirmation of Skt. terms bhavana, atiramya

sources.title: Dilwara Temples — Bharatpedia
sources.consultedFor: Sequence of the Ābu temples: Vimal Vasahi (1031, Ādinātha) then Luna Vasahi (1230, Neminātha) — the 'second' temple of this verse

notes: Historical identification is secure: bīaṁ ('second') Jina-temple 'there' (= Mount Ābu of v.92) built by/for the minister Vastupāla = the white-marble Lūṇa-vasahī of Neminātha, 1230 CE. The hymn credits Vastupāla, but site inscriptions assign the actual foundation to his brother Tejapāla, who dedicated it to their late brother Lūṇiga; the brothers are remembered jointly, so the single name carries the family's merit. Lexical uncertainty centres on (1) maḍhicchiyaṁ — parsed here as maḍha- 'overlay/inlay' (cf. Guj. મઢવું) + icchiya 'desired' ('wrought to the heart's wish'); a reading mano-icchiyaṁ ('mind-desired') is grammatically possible and other editions may vary; and (2) aïramma-maï, taken as the compound atiramya-maya ('made of supreme loveliness') rather than two separate words. sūrakhaṁ is read as sūra (sun, long ū) + kha (sky); a minority sense sura ('gods') would yield 'like the heaven of the gods'. None of these affects the verse's overall sense.
